data.command: echo '--- IDAHO-VAULT CONTEXT INJECTION ---' && echo 'Date:' $(date '+%Y-%m-%d') && echo 'Branch:' $(git branch --show-current) && echo '' && echo 'LEVELSET: Read !/!/LEVELSET-CURRENT.md for current vault state.' && echo 'CLAUDE.md: Loaded automatically — single source of truth for operations.' && echo 'DECISIONS: Governance file at !/DECISIONS.md — read only, do not use as relay surface.' && echo '' && echo 'Vault owner: Logan Finney — journalist, Idaho Reports / Idaho Public Television' && echo 'All committed content is ON THE RECORD and PUBLISHABLE.' && echo '--- END CONTEXT INJECTION ---'

content.text:
Fix the following issues. The issues can be from different files or can overlap on same lines in one file.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @!/!/SESSION-2026-03-29-artifacts/Linear - agent chat - Manage Linear workspace and GitHub PRs.md around lines 30 - 31, The document lists PR #67 and PR #69 as "open pull requests" and also states they are "merged now," which is contradictory; verify the true status of PR #67 and PR #69 on GitHub, then update every occurrence in the document so the status is consistent (replace either the "open pull requests" entries or the "merged now" statement to reflect the verified state), and ensure any related note about "not mergeable right now" is corrected or removed to match the verified status of those PRs.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @!/!/SESSION-2026-03-29-artifacts/RING.md at line 8, Add a short header above the quoted block that states the document purpose (e.g., whether this is reference material, a session prompt, or a metaphor used by the project) and how it relates to the current session or project; explicitly note what the "BELLHOPS" alias represents and add attribution if the quoted passage is from a literary source (or mark as original). Also ensure the file ends with a single trailing newline character. Locate the quoted text beginning with "Ring!" and update surrounding text accordingly so the quote is framed by the new header/context and ends with a newline.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @!/!/SESSION-2026-03-29-artifacts/on fire.md at line 6, Remove the trailing whitespace after the token "[[fire]]" on the final line and ensure the file ends with a single newline character (EOF newline); locate the line containing "[[fire]]" and strip any trailing spaces/tabs and then add a newline at end of file so the file ends with '\n'.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.github/scripts/normalize_budget_data.py around lines 8 - 9, The multi-line with statement opening 'minidata.csv' and 'normalized_budget_data.csv' using "with open(..., ) , open(..., ):" is a syntax error; fix it by grouping the context managers correctly—either wrap the open(...) calls in parentheses so the with statement reads "with (open(...), open(...)):" or use a backslash for line continuation after the first open call—ensure the context variables infile and outfile remain the same and the encoding/newline args are preserved.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.github/workflows/linear-brief.yml around lines 86 - 101, The payload is scrubbed by scrub() before the webhook secret is read, so "webhook_secret" gets removed and validation (in the repository_dispatch branch using provided_secret and hmac.compare_digest) always fails; fix by reading/extracting provided_secret = payload.get("webhook_secret", "") (and normalizing it to a string) from the original payload before calling scrub(), then proceed with scrub(payload) and the existing logic that computes live_write and compares provided_secret to configured_secret (LINEAR_BRIEF_WEBHOOK_SECRET) using hmac.compare_digest; ensure the repository_dispatch branch still converts payload_live_write strings to booleans as before.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.github/workflows/linear-brief.yml around lines 127 - 129, The heredoc delimiter "EOF" can collide with a line in sanitized_payload; change the fh.write calls to use a unique delimiter: generate a marker (e.g., using uuid.uuid4() or a RANDOM suffix) into a variable (e.g., marker = f"EOF_SANITIZED_PAYLOAD_{uuid}") and then write the heredoc using that marker before and after sanitized_payload (fh.write(marker + "\n"), fh.write(sanitized_payload + "\n"), fh.write(marker + "\n")) so the multiline block cannot be prematurely terminated; update the code locations that call fh.write("sanitized_payload<<EOF\n") and the matching tail to use this generated marker and ensure newlines are preserved.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/community-plugins.json at line 11, The entry "obsidian-local-rest-api" exposes the vault via a local REST API and requires a security review; either remove it from the plugin list if not required or add verification documentation: confirm necessity for the workflow, record the authentication/access-control configuration (e.g., required auth method, allowed origins, firewall/local-only settings), note any vault governance approvals, and link to the security review decision so reviewers can validate that "obsidian-local-rest-api" has been assessed and approved or removed.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/community-plugins.json around lines 8 - 14, Update the PR description or commit message to explicitly state which agent made the change to the shared .obsidian configuration and confirm whether LOGAN approved it, and justify the inclusion of each listed plugin ("better-search-views", "obsidian-git", "obsidian-importer", "obsidian-local-rest-api", "mcp-tools", "smart-connections", "templater-obsidian"); if approval is missing, either obtain LOGAN's explicit sign-off or move the changes into the agent-specific dotfolder and remove them from the shared .obsidian list.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/smart-connections/styles.css around lines 172 - 173, The CSS rule contains duplicate font-size declarations — "font-size: var(--font-text-size);" and "font-size: 0.88rem;"; remove one of these duplicate properties so only the intended value remains (either keep the CSS variable for theming or the explicit 0.88rem value), ensuring the selector no longer has two font-size lines to avoid confusion.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/smart-connections/styles.css around lines 708 - 716, Remove the redundant display declaration in the .sc-typing-indicator rule by deleting the first "display: flex" so the default state is "display: none" and the ".sc-typing-indicator.visible" selector is solely responsible for switching to "display: flex"; update the .sc-typing-indicator block to only include padding and alignment (align-items: center; padding: 12px 16px;) and keep the "&.visible { display: flex; }" rule unchanged.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/smart-connections/styles.css around lines 543 - 551, Remove the duplicate margin declaration in the .sc-tool-calls-container rule: delete one of the two margin lines so only the intended spacing remains (either keep "margin: 0.5rem 0;" or "margin: 10px;") and make it consistent with the pattern used for .sc-context-container to avoid the later rule silently overriding the earlier one.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/smart-connections/styles.css around lines 480 - 488, The .sc-context-container rule contains duplicate margin declarations (margin: 0.5rem 0; and margin: 10px;) — remove one to avoid confusion and ensure intended spacing; locate the .sc-context-container CSS selector and delete the redundant margin line (keep whichever value is desired, e.g., delete margin: 0.5rem 0; if you want uniform 10px spacing, or delete margin: 10px; to preserve the vertical spacing of 0.5rem 0).

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/smart-connections/styles.css around lines 373 - 394, The .sc-config-error-notice rule uses hardcoded colors; update it to use theme CSS variables (e.g. replace background-color, border color and text color with variables such as var(--sc-error-bg, #ffcccc), var(--sc-error-border, #ff0000) and var(--sc-error-foreground, #ff0000)) so it adapts to themes while keeping the current hex values as fallbacks; leave the layout selectors (span, button, padding, width, border-radius, font-size/weight) intact and only swap the three color properties to the variables named above (or to existing app variables like --interactive-danger / --background-modifier-error if preferred).

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/templater-obsidian/main.js at line 17, The code currently executes user vault scripts via raw evaluation (affecting ji / generate_user_script_functions / user_script_functions.generate_object when plugin.settings.user_scripts_folder is enabled), which allows arbitrary code execution; replace direct window.eval usage with a hardened flow: (1) refuse to evaluate unless the script path is explicitly allowlisted and the file's integrity hash matches a stored/verified checksum, (2) load the script via a safer mechanism such as creating a Blob and using dynamic import() as a module (or an isolated iframe) instead of eval to limit global scope, and (3) surface an explicit UX trust prompt before first-run to record consent and store the integrity hash; update ji.generate_user_script_functions / ji.generate_object to perform allowlist & hash checks and to import modules via blob/import rather than eval.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/templater-obsidian/main.js at line 16, The HTTP status check in getRequest is using if (t.status < 200 && t.status >= 300) which is impossible; change the condition to reject non-2xx responses (e.g. if (t.status < 200 || t.status >= 300) or equivalently if (!(t.status >= 200 && t.status < 300))) and ensure getRequest throws or returns an error (or rejects the Promise) when this condition is true so 4xx/5xx responses are treated as failures instead of successes.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/templater-obsidian/styles.css around lines 91 - 95, The .cm-s-obsidian .templater-command-bg rule sets left and right but no position, so those properties do nothing; either add an appropriate positioning (e.g., set position: absolute or position: relative on .cm-s-obsidian .templater-command-bg or its positioned ancestor if you intend it to stretch horizontally) or remove the left/right declarations if no positioning behavior is needed; update the .cm-s-obsidian .templater-command-bg CSS rule accordingly.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.obsidian/plugins/templater-obsidian/styles.css around lines 206 - 226, Replace the hardcoded colors in the CodeMirror hint rules so they respect Obsidian themes: update the rules targeting .CodeMirror-hint and li.CodeMirror-hint-active to use existing CSS variables (e.g. --background-primary-alt or --background-primary for hint background, --text-normal or --text-muted for hint text, and --interactive-accent or --accent for the active hint background) instead of literal "white", "black", and "#08f"; keep existing padding/margins and cursor behavior but swap color values to the theme variables to restore dark-mode compatibility.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @.vscode/settings.json around lines 3 - 14, The test settings contain invalid placeholder arguments: remove the standalone "!" from python.testing.pytestArgs and the "./!" from python.testing.unittestArgs and replace them with valid values (e.g., an empty array [] if you want default discovery, or a real test directory like "tests") so pytest and unittest discovery won't fail; update the keys python.testing.pytestArgs and python.testing.unittestArgs accordingly to only include valid CLI args or paths.

- Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In @2021-11-03 - Microsoft - Troubleshoot Terminal launch failures.md around lines 26 - 33, The list under terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.{platform} incorrectly indents sibling settings as sub-bullets; unindent the items terminal.integrated.profiles.{platform}, terminal.integrated.cwd, terminal.integrated.env.{platform}, terminal.integrated.inheritEnv, terminal.integrated.automationProfile.{platform}, terminal.integrated.splitCwd, and terminal.integrated.windowsEnableConpty so they appear at the same bullet level as terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.{platform}, preserving the order and punctuation but removing the extra nesting to reflect they are sibling settings.

---
date created: Sunday, March 29th 2026, 10:47:48 am
date modified: Sunday, March 29th 2026, 10:48:33 am
---

# Manage Linear workspace and GitHub PRs

## User

Manage Linear internal workspace and GitHub open PRs

## Assistant

I can help manage the Linear workspace and any pull requests that are already synced into Linear. What do you want me to handle first—open issues, project status, pull request review queue, or something else?

## User

There are several open PRs that can't be merged. The new updates aren't getting reflected in Linear as far as I can tell. Also, the desktop agent is working out of a "swarm" folder that doesn't appear on the online GitHub page and contains files that ought to belong in the "!" swarm nest.

## Assistant

I’m checking the open pull requests in your workspace first, then I’ll compare that with what Linear is showing so I can pinpoint whether this is a sync issue or just missing linkage.

I found two pull requests that are still open in Linear, and both are currently marked as blocked rather than missing. I’m checking the GitHub/Linear integration behavior next so I can separate an actual sync problem from changes that simply never reached the repo.

Linear is seeing the two currently open pull requests, and both are marked **blocked**, so this doesn’t look like a case where the PRs are missing from Linear.

Found 2 open pull requests.

* [Refresh THE COURTROOM docket for LAF-7 coordination](https://linear.app/loganfinney/review/refresh-the-courtroom-docket-for-laf-7-coordination-e67e84aec3f5)
* [Break out THE COURTROOM into scoped project tracks](https://linear.app/loganfinney/review/break-out-the-courtroom-into-scoped-project-tracks-24f708144c22)

What I’m seeing:

* The open PRs are in Linear, but they’re not mergeable right now.
* Linear’s PR view is mostly read-only; merge/approval work still happens in GitHub, and those results are then reflected back into Linear.
* The local desktop-agent `swarm` folder issue is probably separate from Linear sync. If that folder only exists locally and hasn’t been committed/pushed to the repo, neither GitHub nor Linear will show it.
* Your note about files belonging in the `!` swarm nest lines up with an already merged cleanup PR that consolidated swarm coordination files, so the local agent may be working from an outdated or divergent local checkout rather than the repo’s current structure.

Possible next steps:

* I audit the two blocked PRs against their linked issues in Linear.
* I map the current swarm-related issues and PR history to the intended `!` swarm nest structure.
* You paste the exact local path/tree the desktop agent is using, and I’ll compare it against what Linear and GitHub currently reflect.

## User

How fares the swarm?

## Assistant

I’m pulling the current swarm-related issues and pull requests so I can give you a clean status read rather than a guess.

The swarm looks **stable in Linear**. The core swarm work I found is done: [LAF-7](https://linear.app/loganfinney/issue/LAF-7/swarm-coordination-agent-assembly-at-the-courtroom), [LAF-12](https://linear.app/loganfinney/issue/LAF-12/break-out-the-courtroom-into-project-scoped-work-items), and [LAF-10](https://linear.app/loganfinney/issue/LAF-10/define-mvp-swarm-workflow-across-vault-linear-and-slack) are all completed.

The strongest signal on the folder question is that the repo already absorbed the cleanup:

* [PR #58](https://linear.app/loganfinney/review/refactor-consolidate-swarm-nest-home-all-agent-coordination-files-88d72aa2ced8) consolidated the swarm nest
* [PR #72](https://linear.app/loganfinney/review/move-swarm-into-to-contain-swarm-infrastructure-within-the-ops-zone-dd16959675bc) moved `swarm/` into `!/`
* [PR #67](https://linear.app/loganfinney/review/refresh-the-courtroom-docket-for-laf-7-coordination-e67e84aec3f5) and [PR #69](https://linear.app/loganfinney/review/break-out-the-courtroom-into-scoped-project-tracks-24f708144c22) are also merged now

So overall, the swarm itself fares well; the mismatch you noticed looks more like a **local desktop-agent checkout drifting from the current GitHub repo**, not an active Linear-side blockage. Possible next steps:

* I trace the repo/Linear history into a single “intended swarm layout” note.
* You paste the local directory tree from the desktop agent, and I’ll pinpoint exactly what’s out of place.

## User

Can I archive your conversations?

## Assistant

I’m checking the product docs for how conversation history can be kept or hidden so I can answer this accurately.

Not currently, as far as the docs show. Linear keeps chat history so you can return to past conversations, but the documented controls mention browsing recent chats and starting a new chat for a clean slate—not archiving chats.

## User

Would you like to persist as a vaulted entity in the unified swarm?

## Assistant

I don’t persist as an autonomous vaulted entity, but I can help you store the useful parts of this work inside Linear.

Possible next steps:

* Save the operating pattern from this chat as a reusable skill
* Create a document that captures the unified swarm state, norms, and folder model
* Create an issue to track the local-vs-repo swarm divergence and cleanup work

## User

Who are "you" and who am I?

## Assistant

I’m Linear, your workspace assistant for this repo and workspace context.

You’re Logan Finney, working in the [Logan Finney](https://linear.app/loganfinney/team/LAF/all) team on the [IDAHO-VAULT](https://linear.app/loganfinney/project/idaho-vault-df3c1d3e366e) effort.

## User

You can write and delegate to other agents however?

## Assistant

I’m checking the available issue actions in this workspace so I can answer that precisely.

Yes. I can write issues, update them, and delegate issues to other agents in Linear.

What I can do:

* create an issue and assign or delegate it
* update an existing issue’s status, assignee, priority, project, labels, or description
* delegate an issue directly to an agent
* delegate with extra instructions when you want the agent to focus on a specific approach

I can’t directly make a separate agent persist independently as its own long-lived entity, but I can route work to agents through issues.

1→---
     2→source: "https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch"
     3→author:
     4→  - "[[Microsoft]]"
     5→published: 2021-11-03
     6→created: 2026-03-29
     7→---
     8→
     9→To start, we'd like to say we're sorry that you're here reading this document instead of having a good time using the Integrated Terminal in Visual Studio Code. The VS Code team works hard to make the terminal experience as seamless as possible but in some cases, there are issues with shell or terminal configurations that the VS Code editor can't work around.
    10→
    11→After having worked with hundreds of developers to diagnose their terminal launch failures, the VS Code team put together this article with the advice and troubleshooting tips that have helped people in the past. We hope you can find an answer here for your shell or terminal issue and can get back to work quickly.
    12→
    13→## Integrated Terminal user guide
    14→
    15→If you are new to using the VS Code Integrated Terminal, you can learn more in the [Integrated Terminal](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/basics) user guide. There you can read how to [configure](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/profiles) the terminal, and review answers to [common questions](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/basics#_common-questions).
    16→
    17→Below are specific troubleshooting steps, if the user guide hasn't helped you diagnose the launch failure. The troubleshooting steps, such as checking your settings and enabling logging, apply to all platforms that support VS Code: macOS, Linux, and Windows.
    18→
    19→> **Note**: If you're on Windows, you might want to review the [common issues on Windows](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch#_common-issues-on-windows) section first.
    20→
    21→## Troubleshooting steps
    22→
    23→To troubleshoot Integrated Terminal launch failures in Visual Studio Code, follow these steps to diagnose issues:
    24→
    25→1. **Check your user settings.** Review these `terminal.integrated` [settings](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/configure/settings) that could affect the launch:
    26→	- `terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.{platform}` - The default shell profile that the terminal uses.
    27→	- `terminal.integrated.profiles.{platform}` - The defined shell profiles. Sets the shell path and arguments.
    28→	- `terminal.integrated.cwd` - The current working directory (cwd) for the shell process.
    29→	- `terminal.integrated.env.{platform}` - Environment variables that are added to the shell process.
    30→	- `terminal.integrated.inheritEnv` - Whether new shells should inherit their environment from VS Code.
    31→	- `terminal.integrated.automationProfile.{platform}` - Shell profile for automation-related terminal usage like tasks and debug.
    32→	- `terminal.integrated.splitCwd` - Controls the current working directory a split terminal starts with.
    33→	- `terminal.integrated.windowsEnableConpty` - Whether to use ConPTY for Windows terminal process communication.
    34→	You can review settings in the Settings editor (**File** > **Preferences** > **Settings**) and search for specific settings by the setting ID.
    35→	![Search for Integrated terminal settings](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/search-for-settings.png)
    36→	Search for Integrated terminal settings
    37→	A quick way to check if you have changed settings that you might not be aware of, is to use the `@modified` filter in the Settings editor.
    38→	![Filter for modified settings](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/search-for-modified-settings.png)
    39→	Filter for modified settings
    40→	Most Integrated Terminal settings need to be modified directly in your user `settings.json` JSON file. You can open `settings.json` via the **Edit in settings.json** link in the Settings editor, or with the **Preferences: Open User Settings (JSON)** command from the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P).
    41→	![A user's settings.json file](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/settings-json-file.png)
    42→	A user's settings.json file
    43→2. **Test your shell directly.** Try running your designated integrated terminal shell outside VS Code from an external terminal or command prompt. Some terminal launch failures might be due to your shell installation and are not specific to VS Code. The exit codes displayed come from the shell and you might be able to diagnose shell issues by searching on the internet for the specific shell and exit code.
    44→3. **Use the most recent version of VS Code.** Each VS Code weekly release has many updates and fixes and might include integrated terminal improvements. You can check your VS Code version via **Help** > **About** (on macOS **Code** > **About Visual Studio Code**). To find the latest version of VS Code, go to the VS Code [release notes](https://code.visualstudio.com/updates). You might also want to check that you have installed the latest version of your shell.
    45→4. **Use the most recent version of your shell.** If your shell is installed separate from your platform, try installing the latest available version of the shell. The same advice applies if you are on an older build of your operating system. For example, some older versions of Windows 10 did not work well with the VS Code terminal.
    46→5. **Enable trace logging.** You can enable [trace logging](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Terminal-Issues#enabling-trace-logging) and capture a log when launching the terminal. Logging often reveals what is wrong as all arguments used to create the terminal process/pty are recorded. Bad shell names, arguments, or environment variables can cause the terminal to not launch. Keep this log for later if your problem isn't solved.
    47→
    48→## Additional troubleshooting steps
    49→
    50→If none of these steps helped solve the issue, you can also try:
    51→
    52→- Ask about it on [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/), often launch issues are related to environment setup and not a problem with VS Code.
    53→- If the terminal is being launched from an extension, report the issue to the extension by opening the issue reporter (Help > Report Issue) and set File On = "An Extension"
    54→- If you believe it to be a bug with VS Code, report the issue using the issue reporter (**Help** > **Report Issue**). The issue reporter autofills relevant information, see [Creating great terminal issues](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Terminal-Issues#creating-great-terminal-issues) for what else to include in the report.
    55→- If you're on Windows 10 1809 (build 17763) or below, the issue is related to the legacy "winpty" backend. Upgrading to Windows 1903 (build 18362) moves you onto the new "conpty" backend that is built by Microsoft and could fix your problem.
    56→- If your terminal is set to run as administrator only, and you are not launching VS Code as administrator, the terminal is not able to open. You can either change the default terminal or edit the properties of the terminal exe to not run as administrator.
    57→
    58→## Exit codes
    59→
    60→The exit codes displayed in the terminal launch failure notification are returned from the shell process and are not generated by VS Code. There are many available shells that can be used in the terminal and hundreds of possible exit codes.
    61→
    62→- Try searching on the internet for your specific shell and exit code (for example, "PowerShell 4294901760") and you might find specific suggestions or known issues related to your terminal launch failure.
    63→- Try searching in your shell's issue repository. For example, if you are having trouble with WSL, you might find a workaround searching for your error code in the open or resolved issues at [https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues).
    64→
    65→## Common issues on Windows
    66→
    67→### Make sure compatibility mode is disabled
    68→
    69→When you upgrade to Windows 10, some apps might have compatibility mode turned on automatically. If compatibility mode is enabled for VS Code, the terminal breaks because it does some low-level things to enable the emulation it uses. You can check and disable compatibility mode by right-clicking on the VS Code executable, select **Properties**, and then uncheck the **Run this program in compatibility mode** option in the **Compatibility** tab.
    70→
    71→### The terminal exited with code 1 on Windows 10 (with WSL as the default shell)
    72→
    73→This error can happen if Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) is not set up with a valid default Linux distribution.
    74→
    75→**Note:** 'docker-desktop-data' is not a valid distribution.
    76→
    77→- Open PowerShell and enter `wslconfig.exe /l` to confirm WSL is installed correctly and list the currently available Linux distributions within your system. Confirm a valid distribution has **(default)** next to it.
    78→- To change the default distribution, enter `wslconfig.exe /setdefault "distributionNameAsShownInList"`
    79→
    80→### A native exception occurred
    81→
    82→Typically this error occurs due to anti-virus software intercepting and blocking the winpty/conpty components from creating the terminal process. To work around this error, you can exclude the following file from your anti-virus scanning:
    83→
    84→```text
    85→{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\winpty.dll
    86→{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\winpty-agent.exe
    87→{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\conpty.node
    88→{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\conpty_console_list.node
    89→```
    90→
    91→Reporting this issue to the Anti-virus team can also help stamp out the issue all together.
    92→
    93→### Terminal exits with code 259
    94→
    95→Exit code **259** can mean `STILL_ACTIVE` when the terminal is trying to start a new process such as PowerShell.exe. You can try killing unused programs and processes on your machine in case one of them is keeping a terminal shell process active and unable to relaunch.
    96→
    97→Anti-virus software running on your machine might also interfere with starting your terminal shell.
    98→
    99→### Terminal exits with code 3221225786 (or similar)
   100→
   101→This can happen when you have legacy console mode enabled in conhost's properties. To change this, open cmd.exe from the start menu, right-click the title bar, go to **Properties** and under the **Options** tab, uncheck **Use legacy console**.
   102→
   103→![Use legacy mode checkbox](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/legacy-console-mode.png)
   104→
   105→- [Integrated Terminal user guide](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/basics) - Learn more about general terminal use and configuration.

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
---
source: "https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch"
author:
  - "[[Microsoft]]"
published: 2021-11-03
created: 2026-03-29
---

To start, we'd like to say we're sorry that you're here reading this document instead of having a good time using the Integrated Terminal in Visual Studio Code. The VS Code team works hard to make the terminal experience as seamless as possible but in some cases, there are issues with shell or terminal configurations that the VS Code editor can't work around.

After having worked with hundreds of developers to diagnose their terminal launch failures, the VS Code team put together this article with the advice and troubleshooting tips that have helped people in the past. We hope you can find an answer here for your shell or terminal issue and can get back to work quickly.

## Integrated Terminal user guide

If you are new to using the VS Code Integrated Terminal, you can learn more in the [Integrated Terminal](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/basics) user guide. There you can read how to [configure](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/profiles) the terminal, and review answers to [common questions](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/basics#_common-questions).

Below are specific troubleshooting steps, if the user guide hasn't helped you diagnose the launch failure. The troubleshooting steps, such as checking your settings and enabling logging, apply to all platforms that support VS Code: macOS, Linux, and Windows.

> **Note**: If you're on Windows, you might want to review the [common issues on Windows](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch#_common-issues-on-windows) section first.

## Troubleshooting steps

To troubleshoot Integrated Terminal launch failures in Visual Studio Code, follow these steps to diagnose issues:

1. **Check your user settings.** Review these `terminal.integrated` [settings](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/configure/settings) that could affect the launch:
	- `terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.{platform}` - The default shell profile that the terminal uses.
	- `terminal.integrated.profiles.{platform}` - The defined shell profiles. Sets the shell path and arguments.
	- `terminal.integrated.cwd` - The current working directory (cwd) for the shell process.
	- `terminal.integrated.env.{platform}` - Environment variables that are added to the shell process.
	- `terminal.integrated.inheritEnv` - Whether new shells should inherit their environment from VS Code.
	- `terminal.integrated.automationProfile.{platform}` - Shell profile for automation-related terminal usage like tasks and debug.
	- `terminal.integrated.splitCwd` - Controls the current working directory a split terminal starts with.
	- `terminal.integrated.windowsEnableConpty` - Whether to use ConPTY for Windows terminal process communication.
	You can review settings in the Settings editor (**File** > **Preferences** > **Settings**) and search for specific settings by the setting ID.
	![Search for Integrated terminal settings](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/search-for-settings.png)
	Search for Integrated terminal settings
	A quick way to check if you have changed settings that you might not be aware of, is to use the `@modified` filter in the Settings editor.
	![Filter for modified settings](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/search-for-modified-settings.png)
	Filter for modified settings
	Most Integrated Terminal settings need to be modified directly in your user `settings.json` JSON file. You can open `settings.json` via the **Edit in settings.json** link in the Settings editor, or with the **Preferences: Open User Settings (JSON)** command from the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P).
	![A user's settings.json file](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/settings-json-file.png)
	A user's settings.json file
2. **Test your shell directly.** Try running your designated integrated terminal shell outside VS Code from an external terminal or command prompt. Some terminal launch failures might be due to your shell installation and are not specific to VS Code. The exit codes displayed come from the shell and you might be able to diagnose shell issues by searching on the internet for the specific shell and exit code.
3. **Use the most recent version of VS Code.** Each VS Code weekly release has many updates and fixes and might include integrated terminal improvements. You can check your VS Code version via **Help** > **About** (on macOS **Code** > **About Visual Studio Code**). To find the latest version of VS Code, go to the VS Code [release notes](https://code.visualstudio.com/updates). You might also want to check that you have installed the latest version of your shell.
4. **Use the most recent version of your shell.** If your shell is installed separate from your platform, try installing the latest available version of the shell. The same advice applies if you are on an older build of your operating system. For example, some older versions of Windows 10 did not work well with the VS Code terminal.
5. **Enable trace logging.** You can enable [trace logging](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Terminal-Issues#enabling-trace-logging) and capture a log when launching the terminal. Logging often reveals what is wrong as all arguments used to create the terminal process/pty are recorded. Bad shell names, arguments, or environment variables can cause the terminal to not launch. Keep this log for later if your problem isn't solved.

## Additional troubleshooting steps

If none of these steps helped solve the issue, you can also try:

- Ask about it on [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/), often launch issues are related to environment setup and not a problem with VS Code.
- If the terminal is being launched from an extension, report the issue to the extension by opening the issue reporter (Help > Report Issue) and set File On = "An Extension"
- If you believe it to be a bug with VS Code, report the issue using the issue reporter (**Help** > **Report Issue**). The issue reporter autofills relevant information, see [Creating great terminal issues](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Terminal-Issues#creating-great-terminal-issues) for what else to include in the report.
- If you're on Windows 10 1809 (build 17763) or below, the issue is related to the legacy "winpty" backend. Upgrading to Windows 1903 (build 18362) moves you onto the new "conpty" backend that is built by Microsoft and could fix your problem.
- If your terminal is set to run as administrator only, and you are not launching VS Code as administrator, the terminal is not able to open. You can either change the default terminal or edit the properties of the terminal exe to not run as administrator.

## Exit codes

The exit codes displayed in the terminal launch failure notification are returned from the shell process and are not generated by VS Code. There are many available shells that can be used in the terminal and hundreds of possible exit codes.

- Try searching on the internet for your specific shell and exit code (for example, "PowerShell 4294901760") and you might find specific suggestions or known issues related to your terminal launch failure.
- Try searching in your shell's issue repository. For example, if you are having trouble with WSL, you might find a workaround searching for your error code in the open or resolved issues at [https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues).

## Common issues on Windows

### Make sure compatibility mode is disabled

When you upgrade to Windows 10, some apps might have compatibility mode turned on automatically. If compatibility mode is enabled for VS Code, the terminal breaks because it does some low-level things to enable the emulation it uses. You can check and disable compatibility mode by right-clicking on the VS Code executable, select **Properties**, and then uncheck the **Run this program in compatibility mode** option in the **Compatibility** tab.

### The terminal exited with code 1 on Windows 10 (with WSL as the default shell)

This error can happen if Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) is not set up with a valid default Linux distribution.

**Note:** 'docker-desktop-data' is not a valid distribution.

- Open PowerShell and enter `wslconfig.exe /l` to confirm WSL is installed correctly and list the currently available Linux distributions within your system. Confirm a valid distribution has **(default)** next to it.
- To change the default distribution, enter `wslconfig.exe /setdefault "distributionNameAsShownInList"`

### A native exception occurred

Typically this error occurs due to anti-virus software intercepting and blocking the winpty/conpty components from creating the terminal process. To work around this error, you can exclude the following file from your anti-virus scanning:

```text
{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\winpty.dll
{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\winpty-agent.exe
{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\conpty.node
{install_path}\resources\app\node_modules.asar.unpacked\node-pty\build\Release\conpty_console_list.node
```

Reporting this issue to the Anti-virus team can also help stamp out the issue all together.

### Terminal exits with code 259

Exit code **259** can mean `STILL_ACTIVE` when the terminal is trying to start a new process such as PowerShell.exe. You can try killing unused programs and processes on your machine in case one of them is keeping a terminal shell process active and unable to relaunch.

Anti-virus software running on your machine might also interfere with starting your terminal shell.

### Terminal exits with code 3221225786 (or similar)

This can happen when you have legacy console mode enabled in conhost's properties. To change this, open cmd.exe from the start menu, right-click the title bar, go to **Properties** and under the **Options** tab, uncheck **Use legacy console**.

![Use legacy mode checkbox](https://code.visualstudio.com/assets/docs/supporting/troubleshoot-terminal-launch/legacy-console-mode.png)

- [Integrated Terminal user guide](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/terminal/basics) - Learn more about general terminal use and configuration.

[[fire]]
